questions.question: Explain the diamond problem in C++ multiple inheritance with an example.
keyPoints: Diamond problem definition: ambiguity when two classes inherit from same base
Example showing inheritance hierarchy (A → B, A → C, B,C → D)
Problem: duplicate base class members causing ambiguity
Without virtual: two copies of A exist in D

questions.question: What is a virtual base class? How does it solve the diamond problem?
keyPoints: Virtual base class definition using 'virtual' keyword
Only one copy of base class is inherited
vbptr manages single instance
Eliminates ambiguity in member access

questions.question: Explain the constructor call order in a virtual inheritance hierarchy.
keyPoints: Virtual base constructors called first
Called only once regardless of multiple paths
Then direct (non-virtual) base constructors
Finally most-derived class constructor
Order: A → B,C → D

questions.question: How do you resolve ambiguity when accessing members of a virtual base class?
keyPoints: Use scope resolution operator (::)
Syntax: DerivedClass.BaseClass::memberName
Example: obj.B::show() or obj.C::show()
Direct access may also work with virtual base

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating virtual base class to solve diamond problem.
keyPoints: Show class A (base), B:virtual public A, C:virtual public A, D:public B,public C
Define constructor for each class
Show single instance of A in D
Demonstrate accessing A's members without ambiguity

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using virtual base classes?
keyPoints: Advantages: Eliminates diamond problem, single copy of base, saves memory, resolves ambiguity
Disadvantages: Increased object size (vbptr), complexity, performance overhead
Trade-off between correctness and efficiency

questions.question: Who is responsible for initializing the virtual base class and why?
keyPoints: Most-derived class is responsible
Virtual base constructor called only once
Intermediate classes cannot initialize virtual base
Ensures proper initialization of shared base

questions.question: Design and implement a complete C++ program with diamond inheritance using virtual base classes. Include appropriate constructors, show the order of execution, and demonstrate that only one copy of the base class exists.
keyPoints: Define base class with constructor showing message
Define intermediate classes with virtual inheritance
Define derived class with public B,C inheritance
Main function creating object and observing output
Constructor order output: A first, then B,C, then D
Proof of single base instance through sizeof or member access

questions.question: Explain with a diagram the memory layout difference between non-virtual and virtual inheritance in diamond problem. Also write code to demonstrate the difference in object size.
keyPoints: Diagram showing two A subobjects without virtual
Diagram showing one shared A subobject with virtual
Code using sizeof() to show size difference
Explanation of vbptr overhead
Discussion on when to use virtual inheritance

questions.question: What is the role of the most-derived class in virtual inheritance? Explain constructor order with an example. Also explain what happens if the most-derived class does not explicitly call the virtual base constructor.
keyPoints: Most-derived class initializes virtual base
Constructor order explanation with example
If not explicit: default constructor called
With explicit: specified constructor called
Demonstrate with code showing different scenarios

questions.question: Write a C++ program to create a class hierarchy representing: Person (base), Student and Teacher virtually inherit from Person, and TeachingAssistant virtually inherits from both Student and Teacher. Implement appropriate data members and member functions. Demonstrate the diamond problem solution.
keyPoints: class Person with name, age
class Student : virtual public Person
class Teacher : virtual public Person
class TeachingAssistant : public Student, public Teacher
Single copy of Person in TeachingAssistant
Access Person's members without ambiguity
